coredata.dc:title: Ultrasound-assisted starch hydrolyzing by alpha-amylase: Implementation of computational fluid dynamics, acoustic field determination, and rheology modeling
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Roohi, Reza
Abedi, Elahe
Mohammad Bagher Hashemi, Seyed
coredata.dc:description: The study aimed to optimize the ultrasonic-assisted modification (UAM) of corn and potato starch by assessing the influence of ultrasound geometry, power, and frequency on the fluid flow for sonicated starch to achieve porous starch with a higher degree of hydrolyzing by α-amylase. This assessment was conducted through mathematical modeling and 3D computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations. The ultrasonic pressure field is determined by the solution of the non-linear Westervelt equation in the frequency domain. Then, the obtained field is utilized to simulate the dissipated power and flow field characteristics. According to the results obtained from the Rapid Visco Analyzer (RVA), it was observed that the peak and final viscosity of hydrolyzed sonicated starch were less than hydrolyzed native starch. This decrease in viscosity indicates a breakdown of the starch structure, leading to a more fluid-like consistency. The shear rate and shear stress data are used for rheology modeling. The fluid's viscosity is represented based on three models of Herschel–Bulkley, Casson, and Power law (Ostwald–de Waele). The magnitude of yield shear stress at low shear rates, the shear-thinning behavior, and the nearly Newtonian fluid nature at high shear rates are extracted from the viscosity models. The surfaces of the starch granules were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) revealed that sonication treatments caused damage, cracks, and porosity on the surfaces of the starch granules which were prone to amylolytic enzymes. This indicates that the structural integrity of the granules was compromised and facilitated enzyme penetration. This study proposes that ultrasonication can be utilized to produce damaged starch, which is susceptible to hydrolysis by α-amylase. This approach holds the potential for reducing enzyme consumption in various industries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Herschel–Bulkley model
Casson model
Power law model
CFD simulation
Rheology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of the examined domain and b) the computational grids.
The contours of the acoustic pressure field at the plane passing through the center of the starch containers.
The contours of a) total power dissipation density (W/m3), b) sound pressure level (dB) and c) intensity magnitude (W/m2) at the plane passing through the center of the starch containers.
The contours of a) total power dissipation density (W/m3), b) sound pressure level (dB) and c) intensity magnitude (W/m2) at the plane passing through the center of the starch containers.

The variation of the shear stress as a function of RVA (A) and shear rate (B) of native (P; potato and C; corn), ultrasonicated (PU; ultrasonicated potato and CU; ultrasonicated corn), and hydrolyzed potato and corn starch (P1, C1, PU1 and CU1). The SEM and pasting graphs are from Pourmohammadi et al. (2023) [2].
The implemented structural parameters and operational conditions.
The power law fluid model parameters.

head.ce:title: Ultrasound-assisted starch hydrolyzing by alpha-amylase: Implementation of computational fluid dynamics, acoustic field determination, and rheology modeling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Fasa University, Fasa, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Fasa University, Fasa, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Technology, Faculty of Agriculture, Fasa University, Fasa, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Technology, Faculty of Agriculture, Fasa University, Fasa, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study aimed to optimize the ultrasonic-assisted modification (UAM) of corn and potato starch by assessing the influence of ultrasound geometry, power, and frequency on the fluid flow for sonicated starch to achieve porous starch with a higher degree of hydrolyzing by α-amylase. This assessment was conducted through mathematical modeling and 3D computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations. The ultrasonic pressure field is determined by the solution of the non-linear Westervelt equation in the frequency domain. Then, the obtained field is utilized to simulate the dissipated power and flow field characteristics. According to the results obtained from the Rapid Visco Analyzer (RVA), it was observed that the peak and final viscosity of hydrolyzed sonicated starch were less than hydrolyzed native starch. This decrease in viscosity indicates a breakdown of the starch structure, leading to a more fluid-like consistency. The shear rate and shear stress data are used for rheology modeling. The fluid's viscosity is represented based on three models of Herschel–Bulkley, Casson, and Power law (Ostwald–de Waele). The magnitude of yield shear stress at low shear rates, the shear-thinning behavior, and the nearly Newtonian fluid nature at high shear rates are extracted from the viscosity models. The surfaces of the starch granules were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) revealed that sonication treatments caused damage, cracks, and porosity on the surfaces of the starch granules which were prone to amylolytic enzymes. This indicates that the structural integrity of the granules was compromised and facilitated enzyme penetration. This study proposes that ultrasonication can be utilized to produce damaged starch, which is susceptible to hydrolysis by α-amylase. This approach holds the potential for reducing enzyme consumption in various industries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The present paper has been approved by all named authors.
The present paper, which is original, has not been published before and is not currently being considered for publication elsewhere.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

ce:section.ce:para: The amylolytic endoenzyme α-amylase (α-1,4 glucan-4-glucanohydrolase, EC 3.2.1.1) functions by hydrolyzing the internal α-1,4-glycosidic bonds found within amylose and amylopectin chains. Enzymatic methods are commonly employed for the liquefaction and saccharification of starch, resulting in the production of malto-oligosaccharides with different chain lengths and dextrins that contain α-(1–6) bonds. These enzymatic processes are widely utilized in large-scale applications, particularly in industries such as food, pharmaceutical, paper, detergent, and textile [1–8]. To date, various enzymatic methods have been utilized to modify the structure of starch. These methods have been investigated and documented by [9–16].

Ultrasonication is a beneficial technique for physically modifying starch, offering several advantages. Firstly, it reduces the need for chemicals and decreases processing time. Additionally, ultrasonication provides high selectivity and quality in the modification process. Moreover, it serves as an environmentally friendly method of processing starch [17,18]. Sonication of starch leads to alterations in its physicochemical attributes, including changes in swelling capacity, solubility, gelatinization temperature and enthalpy, depolymerization or polymerization patterns, and pasting behavior. In this regard, dual-frequency ultrasound has been observed to produce more significant modifications in the structure and characteristics of starch due to its enhanced cavitation yield and faster bubble collapse compared to single-frequency ultrasound [19–21].

Ultrasonication can be utilized either on its own (single modification) or in combination with other techniques (dual modification) to modify the structure of starch. This combination approach allows for more comprehensive and tailored modifications to be made to the starch structure [22,23]. Enzymatic modification of native starch granules is hindered by their semi-crystalline structure, reducing efficiency. To achieve more effective amylolysis using α-amylase, a technique called ultrasonication pre-treatment is employed. This method involves subjecting the starch to ultrasonication, which causes physical damage to the granules and increases their surface area. The damaged starch resulting from ultrasonication has greater accessibility to α-amylase during amylolysis, leading to improved enzymatic modification efficiency.

The application of CFD can be a valuable tool for gaining insights into the physical aspects of the sonication process. However, simulating the complex nature of sonication and the associated cavitation phenomena numerically presents several challenges and complexities.

One of the main challenges in simulating sonication using computational fluid dynamics is determining the boundary conditions for the oscillating surface. Researchers often use a specified pattern of movement for the horn tip as a boundary condition. This method assumes that the transducer tip oscillates at a particular amplitude and frequency. Consequently, a mesh motion boundary condition is applied to introduce vibrations into the fluid region. This approach helps induce the desired effects of sonication in the simulation [24–31]. Additionally, pressure determination is another commonly used boundary condition in CFD studies of sonication. In this method, the amplitude of the pressure generated at the transducer’s tip is estimated based on the sonication power. Then, an oscillating pressure condition is imposed at the corresponding boundary location in the simulation. This approach allows for the incorporation of the effects of pressure waves generated during sonication into the computational model [32,33].

As a result of the indirect contact of the transducer with the fluid can result in non-uniform movement and deflection of the ultrasonic bath. Therefore, it is important to consider the coupling between the transducer and the fluid medium in CFD simulations. This coupling can be achieved by incorporating appropriate boundary conditions and considering the mechanical interaction between the transducer and the fluid. This includes accounting for the transmission of vibrations from the transducer to the fluid and vice versa. By accurately modeling this interaction, the simulation can capture the dynamic behavior of the sonication process more effectively [34–36].

In this study, a comprehensive approach involving mathematical modeling and 3D computational simulations was employed to investigate the impact of bath ultrasound with dual frequency (25 + 40 kHz) on fluid flow characteristics (shear rate and shear stress) during the ultrasonic treatment of potato and corn starch to facilitate amylase hydrolysis. The primary objective was to optimize the ultrasound-assisted starch hydrolyzing by alpha-amylase. The numerical data are incorporated in proper viscosity models to examine the rheology properties of the fluid. By utilizing simulations, the behavior of the system under various operating conditions could be predicted, enabling the identification of optimal process parameters for enhanced efficiency and desired outcomes. This integrated approach provides a deeper understanding of the intricate phenomena associated with ultrasonic treatment and facilitates the development of improved processing techniques for starch hydrolyzing.

The potato and corn starch samples used in this study were provided by Sepahan Co. in Isfahan, Iran. The chemical composition analysis revealed that the native potato starch had a moisture content of 12.2 %, ash content of 0.29 %, protein content of 2.38 %, fat content of 0.11 %, fiber content of 0.16 %, and amylose content of 29.8 %. On the other hand, the corn starch had a moisture content of 13.5 %, ash content of 0.4 %, protein content of 2.1 %, fat content of 0.18 %, fiber content of 0.2 %, and amylose content of 25.4 %. The amylose content was determined using the iodine method as described by Pourmohammadi et al. (2018). The α-amylase enzyme (EC 3.2.1.1) used in the study was sourced from Bacillus licheniformis and purchased from Megazyme, a commercial food-grade preparation company located in Bray, Ireland. The enzyme has an optimum pH of 6.8 and operates at a temperature of 37 °C. All the reagents used in the study were of analytical grade and sourced from Sigma, located in St. Louis, MO, USA. The ultrasound treatment procedure used in the study was adapted from the method described by Yu et al. (2014), with some modifications. To achieve a concentration of 20 % w/w, potato, and corn starches were suspended in an appropriate amount of distilled water were placed in an ultrasonic bath (Pacisa SA, Spain) operating at frequencies of 25 + 40 kHz, with a power output of 300 W. The ultrasound treatment was conducted for 10 min. The ultrasonic bath used in the experiment had a rectangular shape and a volume capacity of 10 L, measuring 30 cm in length, 24 cm in width, and 15 cm in height. The starch samples were placed in a beaker with a volume of 250 mL. The beaker initially contained 100 mL of the sample. The ultrasound treatment was conducted in an ultrasonic bath for 15 min at 45 °C. In addition to three transducers operating at frequencies of 25 + 40 kHz, there were ice bags placed inside the water bath to maintain a temperature of approximately 15 °C during the ultrasound treatment. The acoustic power was 300 W, which was estimated to be around 64.5 ± 2 W using a calorimetric method. The equation used to calculate the power (P) is as follows:
P
W
= m · Cp
dT / dt
where m denotes the mass of liquid (kg), Cp is the liquid heat capacity (J/kgK), and (dT/dt) shows the rate rise in the temperature (K/s).

Native and ultrasonicated corn and potato starch (20 % w/v g each, dry basis) were combined with 50 mM sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.5, containing 0.02 % sodium azide). This experimental procedure was conducted to investigate the effects of ultrasonication on the properties of the starches in the presence of a specific buffer solution. The α-amylases were introduced to the starch slurry at a concentration of 0.15 % (w/w, dry basis, grams of dry starch). The resulting mixture was then placed horizontally in a water bath set at a temperature of 50 °C, with continuous shaking at 150 rpm, to initiate the enzymatic modification process. The enzymatic hydrolysis was allowed to proceed for a duration of 10 h. At the final stage of the modification, varying volumes of anhydrous ethanol were added to the mixture. To terminate the enzymatic reaction, the new mixture was vigorously shaken manually. The modified starch was subsequently washed in two separate replicates using 200 mL of anhydrous ethanol. After the enzymatic modification process, the mixture was subjected to centrifugation at 5000 g for 10 min at a temperature of 40 °C [37]. The resulting starch pellet was then dried overnight to remove any remaining moisture.

The microstructure of native, sonicated, and enzymatically hydrolyzed corn and potato starch samples was analyzed using SEM at an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. The experiments were conducted using a Leica Cambridge instrument located in the UK. To minimize the charging effects that can occur when the samples are exposed to the electron beam, each sample weighing 0.1 g was mounted onto an SEM stub using double-sided adhesive tape. Additionally, a thin layer of gold was sputter-coated onto the surfaces of the samples [38].

To analyze the pasting characteristics of the native, sonicated, and hydrolyzed starches, a RVA from Newport Scientific Pty., Ltd., Warriewood, Australia was utilized. In each RVA canister, 3.5 g of starch (on a dry basis) was accurately weighed, followed by the addition of 25 g of distilled water. To ensure uniform dispersion of the solids in water, each sample was manually mixed for 30 s using the plastic paddle provided with the RVA. Subsequently, the canister was placed in the instrument, and for the initial 20 s, the suspension was stirred at a speed of 960 rpm, while for the remaining duration of the test (23 min), it was stirred at 160 rpm.

The propagation of the ultrasound waves within the fluid is accompanied by contraction and expansion cycles. As the shear stress is negligible compared to the pressure stress in sonication process the governing equation (Helmholtz equation) can be formulated as [39]:
(1)
∇
1 ρ
∇ P

P

2
= 0
where in the above equation ρ , P and C are density, pressure and sound speed, respectively.
r →
and t are representativeof the location vector and time.

Considering the harmonic nature of the pressure waves and the harmonic response of the system boundary conditions (either zero displacement or reflective), the fluid pressure can be mathematically modeled using a harmonic response function. So, the Helmholtz equation can be transformed into Eq. (1) using
P
r →

= P
r →
×
e
i ω t
, where ω is the oscillations frequency [26].
(2)
∇
1 ρ
∇ P
r →
-
ω
2
ρ
C
2
P
r →
= 0
Based on the physical system characteristics, three different boundary conditions are assigned at proper geometry boundaries. At the bottom of the sonication bath, where the transducers are placed, specified pressure (oscillation amplitude) is assigned. The magnitude of the pressure amplitude is determined based on the sonicator power density as:
(3)
I
US
=
P
US
A
=
P
tot
2
2 ρ C
where
P
US
, A and
P
tot
are power, sonicator area and the amplitude of the applied pressure boundary condition. At the fluid free surface, the zero pressure (known as the soft boundary condition) is assigned. For the other solid walls namely container and the transducer’s walls (except of its vibrating surface), the perfect reflector boundary condition is set. For the perfect reflector faces, the normal pressure gradient is set to zero in the calculation process.

The oscillating nature of the ultrasonic waves, results in high amplitude expansion and compression waves, which are responsible for formation micro water vapor bubbles. For the present study, the multiphase mixture model is implemented in addition to Navier-Stokes equations for flow field determination.

The conservation of mass, mixture model and momentum equation are listed hereafter [31]:
(4)
∂
∂ t
ρ m
+ ∇ .

For which the mixture velocity and density are calculated as:
(5)

ρ k
(6)

(7)

where in the above equations, ρ ,

,

and α are density, velocity, pressure, viscosity, gravitational acceleration, external force and mixture volume fraction, respectively. The subscripts of m, k and dr refers to mixture, each individual phases and drift velocity, respectively. The mixture is assumed to have n distinct phases. The mixture viscosity can be estimated using the volumetric average among all phases as:
(8)
μ m

k = 1

μ k
The drift velocity of each phase (
v →
dr , k
) can be calculated as the difference between the velocity of that phase and the mixture.

Besides as the oscillations of pressure are accompanied by phase change between phases, the mass transport model should be implemented. For further discussions regarding the mass transfer process during the sonication process, one could refer to [25].

The rheological models are utilized to predict the flow behavior especially in the shear rates beyond the experimentally extracted data. The rheological models can be categorized in various types based on the influencing parameters such as shear rate, temperature and composition. These models are namely, experimental, theoretical and structural models. The theoretical models used in the current investigation are:

This model which is obtained using a minor tuning of the Bingham model can be categorized as an empirical model for non-Newtonian fluids. The model incorporates three constants of consistency index, degree of shear thickening and thinning and shear stress. The model is particularly suitable for fluids with yield behavior for which the solid properties flowed by fluid properties after a yield stress is occurred [40].
(9)
τ =
τ 0
+ K
γ ̇
n
In the above equation, the stress and yield stress are denoted by τ and
τ 0
and K and n are representatives of consistency and flow behavior indexes.

This model is widely used for the simulation of the relation between shear stress, yield shear stress and shear rate for food and biological materials. Generally, Casson fluids are categorized as shear thinning fluids for which very high viscosities are predictable and small shear rates with the limit of solid behavior at zero shear rates. The flow behavior can be formulated as (Pramanik, 2014):
(10)
τ
=
τ 0
+
η C
γ ̇
where
η C
is the fluid viscosity.

The power law is a simple and widely used model. The model is limited to time-independent non-Newtonian fluids in a specified range of shear rates. However, despite these limitations, due to simplicity, several researchers still implemented the power law model for data fitting to experimental results [40].
(11)
τ = K
γ ̇
n
The n index has a significant influence on the fluid performance at various shear rates. Various viscosity models are fitted using the Origin Pro software.

To determine the pressure field properly, appropriate boundary conditions should be assigned to the domain's peripheral sides. During the numerical simulation of waves passing outwards a mild wave reflection is inevitable. So, it is common to use the Perfectly Matched Layer (PML) boundary condition at the sides where the acoustic waves should leave the domain perfectly [41].

Another main boundary condition that is responsible for delivering the ultrasonic power to the system is the transducers placed at the bottom of the bath container. To simulate their oscillation and the produced acoustic power, their oscillation amplitude should be calculated and applied to the three bottom surfaces of the transducers. The amplitude
Δ
L
max
can be calculated based on the consumed power and the frequency of the system based on the correlation proposed by Abdolhosseinzadeh et al. (2019) as [42]:
(12)
-
n →
.
1
ρ c
∇ p
=
( i ω )
2
Δ
L
max
where in the above equation the normal direction relative to the transducer's surface is denoted by
n →
.
The implemented structural parameters and operational conditions are listed in Table 1 .

The numerical modeling is conducted in three consecutive phases. In the first step, the ultrasonic pressure field is determined by the solution of the non-linear Westervelt equation in the frequency domain. Regarding the reason for Westervelt equation implementation, it should be stated that this equation accounts for non-linear effects in acoustic propagation, which can significantly impact the accuracy of models. It is widely applicable in underwater acoustics, medical imaging, sonar systems, and ultrasonic testing. Compared to other models, it offers a simple formulation that is computationally efficient and easy to implement, making it suitable for real-time simulations. It is particularly effective in modeling high-frequency acoustic waves and accurately captures attenuation and dispersion phenomena, resulting in more accurate results in real-world environments.

Then, the obtained field is utilized to determine the dissipated power and flow field characteristics. Finally, at the third step, the shear rate and shear stress data are used for rheology modeling. The viscosity of the fluid is represented based on three models of Herschel–Bulkley, Casson, and Power law (Ostwald–de Waele).

Numerical simulation of the sonication procedure requires the accurate determination of the acoustic pressure variations within the field. To do so, proper grids should be applied to the computational domain to obtain realistic results. The simulation is performed in two consecutive phases of acoustic pressure and flow field determination. To capture the local fluctuations of the pressure field, the grid sizing for the acoustic simulation should be lowered to about λ/10 (where λ is the wavelength) [43]. A nearly uniform triangular grid is implemented in the entire domain except for PML layers where mapped triangular cell layers are utilized. The schematic of the computational grids is demonstrated in Fig. 1 .
According to the performed grid study, the optimum number of computational cells is determined to be 345,920.

To determine the rheological properties of the examined fluids, the governing equations for acoustic pressure Navier-Stokes are solved simultaneously using the COMSOL MultiphysicsTM 5.5. As the time step for the formation of the acoustic pressure field is significantly lower than the velocity field (due to very high frequency of the ultrasonic transducers), it is reasonable to determine the pressure field in the frequency domain and utilize the obtained values for the determination of the velocity field.

The convergence criteria for velocity and acoustic pressure fields are set to 10-4 and 10-8, respectively. The simulation process is conducted on a computational system with the following hardware specifications: Intel® Xeon® E6540 Processor 2 GHz and 16 GB DDR4 RAM. Each simulation takes an average computation time of 50 min.
In the current section, the results of acoustic pressure field calculations in addition to the rheological modeling of the fluid are presented.

The contours of acoustic pressure waves at a plane passing through the center of the ultrasonic bath are illustrated in Fig. 2 . As it is obvious, the generation of pressure waves near the transducers at the bath bottom, significantly elevates the acoustic pressure at those regions. Moreover, as the waves travel farther from their sources two distinguishable characteristics are observed. First, the periodic nature of compression, and expansion fluctuations, and second, the damping of the sound waves throughout the entire domain. The PML boundaries are responsible for guiding the sound waves outside of the domain artificially to reduce unnecessary numerical complexities. The maximum and minimum pressure amplitude are obtained to be 95.7 and −45.8 kPa, respectively.

The contours of total power dissipation density, sound pressure level, and intensity magnitude at the plane passing through the center of the starch containers are depicted in Fig. 3 . It is worth mentioning that the amount of transferred energy to the fluid has a direct influence on the variation of thermophysical properties including the viscosity. The placement of the starch components above each ultrasonic transducer creates similar pressure and dissipated power patterns (with a slight difference for side containers due to the effect of surrounding walls). It is observed that despite narrow regions within the containers, a uniform distribution of both acoustic ultrasonic pressure and dissipated energy is formed. It is important to note that the level of acoustic intensity directly affects the amount of energy absorbed, and consequently, affects the starch mixture viscosity. The thermal energy produced by the propagation of sound waves in the region can cause the starch molecules to thermally expand and increase in viscosity. However, in certain situations, subjecting the starch to sonication treatment can generate shear forces and agitation, which disrupts the arrangement of molecules and reduces molecular interactions. Therefore, the impact of sonication on the changes in starch viscosity depends on the specific type of starch and needs to be assessed through experimental investigations.

The contours of total power dissipation density, sound pressure level, and intensity magnitude at the plane passing through the center of the ultrasonic bath are illustrated in Fig. 4 . It is obvious that the highest dissipated power density and pressure amplitude occurs near the bottom transducers. However, moving toward the starch containers, a more uniform field of pressure and power variables is observed. The fluid motion due to the applied pressure field and the variation of the thermophysical properties of the fluid as a result of the absorbed ultrasonic, affect the rheological properties which will be discussed in the following.

Fig. 5 clearly illustrates that the surface structure of the sonicated granules treated with dual-frequency ultrasound is significantly different from that of the native granules [2]. Moreover, potato starch is more affected by the treatment compared to corn starch. The literature conducted by Abedi, Pourmohammadi, Jahromi, et al. (2019), Jambrak et al. (2010), and Sujka & Jamroz (2013) support the presence of several structural changes in starch after ultrasonication treatment [38,44,45]. These changes include: 1. Indentations on the granular surface: Ultrasonication leads to the formation of indentations on the surface of starch granules. This effect is more pronounced in potato starch compared to corn starch. 2. Depressions on the granular surface: Ultrasonication also causes the formation of depressions on the surface of starch granules. Similar to indentations, this effect is more prominent in potato starch than in corn starch. 3. Generation of holes, fractures, cracks, and other mechanical damages: The treatment with ultrasonication results in the creation of various types of mechanical damage on the surface of starch granules. These damages include holes, fractures, cracks, and other structural disruptions. Both potato and corn starches exhibit these alterations. 4. Disintegration of granular masses: Ultrasonication causes the rupture of inter- and intra-granule forces, leading to the disintegration of granular masses. This is accompanied by the breakage of polymer chains within the starch structure. The changes in the conformation and structure of starch granules can be attributed to several factors associated with ultrasonication treatment. These factors include: 1. Cavitation bubbles collapse: Ultrasonication generates cavitation bubbles in the liquid medium, which subsequently collapse. The collapse of these bubbles generates localized high-pressure gradients and micro-jetting around the starch granules. 2. Creation of micro-jetting: The collapse of cavitation bubbles creates micro-jetting, which exerts local shear forces on the starch granules. 3. High-pressure gradients: The collapse of cavitation bubbles also generates high-pressure gradients around the starch granules, contributing to the mechanical disruption of their structure. 4. Decomposition of water into radicals: During the collapse of cavitation bubbles, water molecules undergo partial decomposition, resulting in the formation of OH and H radicals within the bubbles. 5. Reaction of radicals with solute molecules: Some of the radicals formed during the collapse of cavitation bubbles disperse into the surrounding liquid medium. These radicals can react with the solute molecules, including starch, leading to structural changes and modifications.

The pasting curves obtained from the RVA for the hydrolyzed, ultrasonicated, and native starch samples are depicted in Fig. 5A. According to [46], several factors can influence these pasting characteristics. These factors include the proportion of amorphous starch, the lengths of amylopectin chains, the leaching of amylose, the swelling of granules, and the resistance between swollen granules. Based on the analysis of the RVA graphs, the sonication procedures significantly decreased the peak viscosity, final viscosity, and pasting temperature of both corn and potato starches. The observed reduction in pasting temperature could be attributed to the fractionation of starch granules caused by cavitation forces during the ultrasound treatment. This fractionation led to increased water permeability of the granules and accelerated gelatinization, ultimately resulting in a lower pasting temperature. The process described above highlights the initial swelling of starch granules during sonication. Ultrasonication can effectively disrupt the hydrogen bonds within and between starch chains, increasing the mobility of these chains. Additionally, ultrasonication facilitates the penetration of more water molecules into the granules before gelatinization occurs, as well as enhances the leaching of amylose from the starch granules. These factors contribute to early gelatinization and a lower pasting temperature. As can be seen in Fig. 5, the CU and PU samples have lower peak and final viscosities compared to the NCS and NPS samples. This observation aligns with the mechanical activation process, which causes the expansion of the amorphous parts of starch granules and weakens the crystalline regions. This characteristic can be explained by the following: the treatment with dual-frequency ultrasound results in significant damage to the starch granules due to shear action. This damage causes the molecules of amylose to straighten out, thereby reducing the shear force between the fluid layers and decreasing viscosity. In general, the hydrogen bonds and crystalline structure present in starch molecules are disrupted during the ultrasonication process. As a result, the structure of starch becomes less rigid, and the molecular weight of amylose and amylopectin is reduced due to the solubilization of starch aggregates and the disintegration of C-C bonds.

Acknowledging the relationship between shear stress and shear rate is crucial when designing and optimizing processes involving materials with complex flow behavior. Understanding the impact of shear stress and shear rate on a material's deformation and flow characteristics can help identify optimal processing conditions, prevent undesired effects such as shear-induced degradation or agglomeration, and ensure the product's quality and performance.

Moreover, the correlation between shear stress and shear rate is essential in various industries and applications. In the food industry, for example, understanding the rheological behavior of ingredients and products is crucial for optimizing processes such as mixing, pumping, and extrusion. Similarly, in the pharmaceutical industry, rheological studies play a critical role in formulating drugs, designing drug delivery systems, and ensuring product stability.

Overall, recognizing the interdependence between shear stress and shear rate is essential for a comprehensive understanding of the flow properties of materials. Neglecting this correlation can limit our ability to accurately characterize and predict the behavior of complex fluids, which can have significant implications for various industries and applications.

The variation of the shear stress as a function of shear rate is illustrated in Fig. 5 for potato and corn native and sonicated starches. A mathematical power function is fitted to the presented data for each case separately with dashed lines (the verification of power-law model incorporation). Regarding the rheological behavior of the fluid, it is observed that the variation rate of shear stress versus shear rate (i.e., viscosity) is higher at low shear rates and gradually reduces, which verifies the non-Newtonian nature of the fluid. In other words, by increasing the shear rate the fluid viscosity is decreased for all of the examined cases which reveals the shear-thinning nature of the fluid.

Besides, focusing on the low range of shear rates, and the possibility of non-zero shear rate at zero shear rate, it is reasonable to assume the existence of yield shear stress. Therefore, the Casson and Herschel–Bulkley models are applied to examine this behavior. Moreover, for high shear rates, a nearly linear relation between the shear stress and shear rate can be observed which dictates a Newtonian performance for this shear rate range [47]. The implementation of the Casson flow model can suitably simulate the mentioned behavior. So, the power law, Casson, and Herschel–Bulkley flow models were fitted to the experimental data. As it is obvious, at each shear rate the corn starch has higher shear stress than the potato starch, and sonication treatment substitutionally decreases the shear stress. The quantification of this observation will be discussed in detail in rheological modeling.

The rheology modeling parameters are listed in Tables 2-4
for the Herschel–Bulkley, Casson, and Power law (Ostwald–de Waele) fluid model. Additionally, based on the obtained values for statistical error, it is obvious that all of the implemented models have excellent compatibility with the flow behavior (the values of R2 and adj-R2 are around 0.99 for all examined cases).

Based on the data presented in Table 2 for the power law model, as the n < 1 criterion is achieved, the fluid including both potato and corn starches in the presence/absence of sonication treatment behaves as a shear-thinning material. The observation means that at low shear rates, higher shear stress is required to maintain the fluid flow (i.e., high viscosity), however at elevated shear rates the viscosity is decreased exponentially.

According to the parameters estimation, the flow index (n) is not significantly affected by the starch type or sonication treatment, in contrary to the consistency parameter (K). In other words, the rate of viscosity variation with shear rate is almost identical in all of the cases with differences in their magnitude. Utilization of the ultrasonic bath, decreases the K parameter for potato and corn starches by 28.3 and 13.8 %, respectively. It should also be added that as the power law assumes negligible viscosity at a zero-shear rate, the effect of yield stress can’t be determined using this model [48].

The data for the Herschel–Bulkley fluid model are listed in Table 3. This model is an extension of the common power law model to account for non-zero shear stress at zero shear rate which is pointed out as the shear yield stress. More precisely, according to this model infinite viscosity (solid-like behavior) is anticipated at near zero shear rates which is reduced rapidly by increasing the shear rate [49]. Based on the Herschel–Bulkley fluid model the effect of potato and corn starches are different subjected to sonication treatment. For potato starch, the yield stress increases by 44.0 % after the utilization of ultrasonic waves while a reduction of 79.5 % is obtained for corn starch. The flow index has a minor range of variation between 0.54 and 0.62 [50]. The model has excellent fitting compatibility with the experimental data similar to the power-law model. However, it should be noted the difference between these two models can be better determined at very low shear rates.

The parameters for the Casson fluid model are represented in Table 4. This two-parameter model incorporates the yield stress assumption (similar to the Herschel–Bulkley fluid model). According to the obtained data, the yield stress is lowered by 20.5 and 27.1 % via utilization of sonication for potato and corn starches, respectively. Moreover, the Casson model implements the assumption of Newtonian behavior for high shear rates. The predicted viscosity is obtained to decrease by 26.1 and 17.0 % subjected to ultrasonic waves for potato and corn starches, respectively. It should be added that focusing on the statistical error, the Casson model has the lowest compatibility with the obtained data in comparison to the other two implemented rheological models.

The disparity in yield shear stress predictions between the Herschel-Bulkley and Casson models can be attributed to their distinct mathematical formulations. The Herschel-Bulkley model assumes a power-law relationship, while the Casson model assumes a square-root relationship. Consequently, the equations take on different forms, yielding varying predictions.

The dissimilarity in these models' predictions can be explained through their respective explanations of how non-Newtonian fluids respond to applied shear. The Herschel-Bulkley model perceives the yield stress as an indication of the fluid's resistance to flow, with the consistency index and flow behavior index dictating its behavior under shear-thinning or shear-thickening conditions. In contrast, the Casson model emphasizes the square root relationship between shear stress and shear rate, assuming that the fluid behaves as a solid until surpassing the yield stress.

Practically, the selection of these models hinges on the specific characteristics of the non-Newtonian fluid under investigation. Each model presents a distinct portrayal of the fluid's behavior, and the choice should be grounded in experimental data and a comprehensive comprehension of the fluid's physical properties.

Mathematical modeling and 3D computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation in addition to ultrasonic pressure field determination is conducted to obtain the dissipated power and flow field characteristics of bath ultrasonicated devices. In the second step, rheological models of native and sonicated starched were measured. Based on the results obtained, it was found that α-amylase was more effective in starches that underwent ultrasonication treatment. This can be attributed to the ability of α-amylase to modify the positioning of intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonds between water and starch molecules. The combination of α-amylase and ultrasonication resulted in significant changes in the properties and characteristics of the starches. At high shear rates, a nearly linear relation between the shear stress and shear rate can be observed which dictates a Newtonian performance for this shear rate range. The implementation of the Casson flow model can suitably simulate the mentioned behavior. At each shear rate, the corn starch has higher shear stress than the potato starch and sonication treatment substitutionally decreases the shear stress. For the power law model, as the n < 1 criterion is achieved, the fluid including both potato and corn starches in the presence/absence of sonication treatment behaves as a shear-thinning material. According to the Herschel–Bulkley fluid model, the flow index has a minor range of variation between 0.54 and 0.62. The model has excellent fitting compatibility with the experimental data similar to the power-law model. For the Casson model, the yield stress is lowered by 20.5 and 27.1 % via utilization of sonication for potato and corn starches, respectively.
Declaration of Competing Interest Statement

In this study, a comprehensive approach involving mathematical modeling and 3D computational simulations was employed to investigate the impact of bath ultrasound with dual frequency (25 + 40 kHz) on fluid flow characteristics (shear rate and shear stress) during the ultrasonic treatment of potato and corn starch to facilitate amylase hydrolysis. The primary objective was to optimize the ultrasound-assisted starch hydrolyzing by alpha-amylase. The numerical data are incorporated in proper viscosity models to examine the rheology properties of the fluid. By utilizing simulations, the behavior of the system under various operating conditions could be predicted, enabling the identification of optimal process parameters for enhanced efficiency and desired outcomes. This integrated approach provides a deeper understanding of the intricate phenomena associated with ultrasonic treatment and facilitates the development of improved processing techniques for starch hydrolyzing.

Elahe Abedi and Reza Roohi wrote the manuscript, conceived and designed the research and analyzed the data. Seyed Mohammad Bagher Hashemi conceived and designed the research and analyzed the data. Elahe Abedi and Reza Roohi conducted the experiments. All the authors read and approved the manuscript.

Reza Roohi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Elahe Abedi: Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Seyed Mohammad Bagher Hashemi: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
